questions.question: Why is hierarchical clustering preferred when working with unknown datasets?
options: a)  It is faster than K-Means
b)  It does not require specifying k beforehand
c)  It handles noise better than all algorithms
d)  It reduces dimensionality automaticallyIn a dendrogram, what indicates that two clusters are very similar?
a)  A long vertical line
b)  A low horizontal merge height
c)  A large number of branches
d)  A steep slopeWhich type of clustering starts with each point as its own cluster?
a)  Divisive
b)  Agglomerative
c)  Density-based
d)  Partition-basedWhy does single linkage produce chain-like clusters?
a)  It maximizes intra-cluster variance
b)  It considers only the closest pair of points
c)  It averages all distances
d)  It ignores outliersWhy is hierarchical clustering considered a greedy algorithm?
a)  It randomly selects clusters
b)  It always picks global optimum
c)  It makes locally optimal merges without revisiting
d)  It minimizes distance globallyWhy is Ward’s method often compared to K-Means?
a)  Both use centroids directly
b)  Both minimize within-cluster variance
c)  Both are density-based
d)  Both avoid distance calculationsYou observe a dendrogram with no large vertical gaps. What does this imply?
a)  Perfect cluster separation
b)  No meaningful clusters exist
c)  Too many clusters exist
d)  Algorithm failedWhich scenario will MOST likely break hierarchical clustering?
a)  Small dataset
b)  Well-separated clusters
c)  Early incorrect merge due to noise
d)  Low dimensional dataWhy is hierarchical clustering not scalable?
a)  It uses recursion
b)  It requires repeated distance matrix updates
c)  It uses centroids
d)  It requires labeled dataWhy is divisive clustering rarely used in practice?
a)  It cannot form clusters
b)  It is less accurate than agglomerative
c)  Optimal splitting is computationally infeasible
d)  It does not use distance metrics
